question: What does Revelation 14:12's description of the saints — 'they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus' — mean? Which commandments does tas entolas tou theou refer to? Is pistis Iesou subjective or objective genitive? How does the Abrahamic pattern work (faith first, obedience after)? How does Paul's 'obedience of faith' connect? Address the legalism objection: Rev 14:12 DESCRIBES the saints (present active participle terountes), not PRESCRIBES conditions.

topics.name: DECALOGUE
topics.entry_text: Written by God EXO 24:12; 31:18; 32:16; DEU 5:22; 9:10; HOS 8:12. Divine authority of EXO 20:1; 34:27,28; DEU 5:4-22. Called WORDS OF THE COVENANT EXO 34:28; DEU 4:13. TABLES OF TESTIMONY EXO 31:18; 34:29; 40:20. See COMMANDMENTS EXO 20:1-17; DEU 5:7-21; MAT 19:18,19; 22:34-40; LUK 10:25-28; ROM 13:8-10.

topics.name: ABRAHAM
topics.entry_text: Instances of faith: Abraham, in forsaking the land of his nativity at the command of God GEN 12:1-4. Believing the promise of many descendants GEN 12:7; 15:4-8. In the offering up of Isaac GEN 22:1-10; ROM 4:18-21; HEB 11:8-19. Instances of obedience: Abraham GEN 12:1-4; 17:23; 21:4; 22:12; NEH 9:8; ACT 7:3-8; HEB 11:8-17; JAS 2:21.

topics.name: PERSEVERANCE
topics.entry_text: See also PATIENCE. General scriptures concerning endurance through trial.

topics.name: LAW
topics.entry_text: See LAW OF MOSES. General scriptures concerning the function of law.

topics.name: COVENANT
topics.entry_text: New covenant passages: JER 31:31-34; HEB 8:6-13; 10:16-18. Law written on hearts, same content, new surface.

topics.name: LOVE
topics.entry_text: Love for God expressed through obedience: DEU 6:5; 10:12; MAT 22:37; MRK 12:30; JHN 14:15; 15:10; 1JN 4:19; 5:3.

topics.name: HOLY SPIRIT
topics.entry_text: Spirit-enablement of obedience: EZK 36:27; ROM 8:4; GAL 5:22,23.

verses.heading: THE crux text — saints identified by commandment-keeping AND faith of Jesus
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here [are] they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: Immediately follows the third angel's warning about beast worship (14:9-11). Closes the hode inclusio opened in 13:10. The saints are DESCRIBED (present active participle terountes), not given a command.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἡ                    ὁ               G3588    [T-NSF] Nom Sg F
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Pl M
τὴν                  ὁ               G3588    [T-ASF] Acc Sg F

verses.notes: Key grammatical features: (1) terountes = V-PAP-NPM = present active participle, nominative plural masculine — descriptive, characterizing the saints by ongoing habitual activity. (2) tas entolas = T-APF N-APF = articular accusative plural — THE commandments, a specific known body. (3) Iesou = N-GSM = genitive singular — the genitive of pistis Iesou is structurally ambiguous (subjective or objective). (4) Contrast with the three aorist imperatives in v.7 (phobethete, dote, proskynesate) — THOSE are prescriptive; v.12 is descriptive.

verses.heading: Parallel identification formula — commandments + testimony of Jesus
verses.kjv_text: And the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ.
verses.context: The dragon, having failed to destroy the woman (Israel/church), turns his fury against the remnant. Twin present active participles identify them: terounton (keeping) and echonton (having).

greek_parsing.gloss: Pres Act Ptcp Gen Pl M
ἐχόντων              ἔχω             G2192    [V-PAP-GPM] Pres Act Ptcp Gen Pl M

verses.notes: Structurally parallel to 14:12. Both use present active participles + tas entolas tou Theou + kai + accusative object with Iesou in genitive. The marturian Iesou construction is structurally identical to pistin Iesou — same genitive ambiguity (subjective: testimony Jesus gives; objective: testimony about Jesus).

verses.heading: Third commandment-keeping blessing — access to tree of life
verses.kjv_text: Blessed [are] they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city.
verses.context: Near the close of Revelation. The blessing is for those who DO (poiountes, present active participle) his commandments. Note poieo (G4160) rather than tereo — broader action word but same descriptive participle pattern.
verses.notes: Third instance of the commandment-keeping identification in Revelation. Completes the triad: 12:17 (keep commandments + testimony), 14:12 (keep commandments + faith), 22:14 (do commandments + access to life). The pairing with the tree of life echoes Genesis — obedience leads to life.

verses.heading: Opening of hode inclusio — patience AND faith of the saints
verses.kjv_text: He that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword. Here is the patience and the faith of the saints.
verses.context: End of the sea beast description (Rev 13:1-10). The hode + estin construction opens the inclusio that 14:12 closes.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἐστιν                εἰμί            G1510    [V-PAI-3S] Pres Act Ind Sg thirdPers
ἡ                    ὁ               G3588    [T-NSF] Nom Sg F

verses.notes: Greek: hode estin he hupomone kai he pistis ton hagion. The construction Hode + estin (present active indicative 3S) is declarative — 'Here IS the patience and the faith.' Content of 14:12 DEFINES what 13:10 introduces: 'patience and faith' = 'commandment-keeping and faith of Jesus.' Both hupomone and pistis carry definite articles (he/he), marking them as specific, recognized qualities.

verses.heading: First angel's everlasting gospel — fear God, give glory, worship Creator
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: The first of three angels with messages. Three aorist imperatives: phobethete (fear), dote (give), proskynesate (worship). These are PRESCRIPTIVE commands — contrasting with the DESCRIPTIVE participle in v.12.
verses.notes: Key parsing: phobethete = V-AOM-2P (aorist passive imperative 2nd plural); dote = V-2AAM-2P (aorist active imperative 2nd plural); proskynesate = V-AAM-2P (aorist active imperative 2nd plural). The creation formula echoes Exo 20:11 LXX through poieo (G4160, aorist active participle dative: poisanti) rather than ktizo. Four shared terms: poieo + ouranos + ge + thalassa.

verses.heading: Third angel's warning — consequence of beast worship
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive [his] mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.
verses.context: The warning immediately preceding v.12's description of the saints. The contrast is stark: those who worship the beast face wrath; those who keep commandments and have faith are the enduring saints.
verses.notes: The juxtaposition of 14:9-11 (beast worshippers) with 14:12 (commandment-keeping saints) creates a sharp binary. The proskyneo contest from 14:7 (worship Creator) vs. 14:9 (worship beast) frames the commandment-keeping as choosing the right object of worship.

verses.heading: Ark of the covenant vision — Decalogue inside the ark
verses.kjv_text: And the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament: and there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail.
verses.context: The seventh trumpet. The heavenly temple is opened, revealing the ark containing the Decalogue. This immediately precedes the dragon-woman narrative (ch. 12) and the first use of 'commandments of God' in 12:17.
verses.notes: The ark contained exclusively the Decalogue tablets (Deut 10:1-5, 1 Kings 8:9). This literary placement frames all subsequent 'commandments of God' references in Rev 12-14 with Decalogue content. The commandment inclusio: 11:19 (ark revealed) -> 12:17 (commandments of God) -> 14:7 (creation formula echoing 4th commandment) -> 14:12 (commandments of God + faith of Jesus).

verses.heading: First link in faith-love-law chain — faith working by love
verses.kjv_text: For in Jesus Christ neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love.
verses.context: Paul's argument that external markers (circumcision) are meaningless in Christ. What matters is pistis di agapes energoumene — faith operating through love.

greek_parsing.gloss: Pres Mid Ptcp Nom Sg F

verses.notes: Greek: pistis (N-NSF) di agapes (N-GSF) energoumene (V-PMP-NSF, present middle participle). The middle voice of energoumene suggests faith is being activated/energized through love. Compare the parallel in 1 Cor 7:19 — same sentence frame: 'neither circumcision nor uncircumcision, but [X].' In Gal 5:6 X = 'faith working by love'; in 1 Cor 7:19 X = 'keeping the commandments of God.' Paul treats them as equivalent expressions.

verses.heading: Second link — love of God = keeping commandments
verses.kjv_text: For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments: and his commandments are not grievous.
verses.context: John defines the love of God in concrete terms: keeping his commandments. Uses the same verb tereo (G5083) as Rev 14:12.
verses.notes: Greek: tas entolas autou teromen (V-PAS-1P, present active subjunctive 1st plural) — 'that we keep his commandments.' Same verb tereo as Rev 14:12. The entolas autou here = God's commandments, same referent as entolas tou Theou in Rev 14:12. John adds 'his commandments are not grievous' (bareiai ouk eisin) — directly countering the legalism charge.

verses.heading: Third link — love fulfills the law
verses.kjv_text: Love worketh no ill to his neighbour: therefore love [is] the fulfilling of the law.
verses.context: Paul has just listed Decalogue commands (v.9) and concludes that love (agape) is the pleroma (fullness/filling-up) of nomos (law).
verses.notes: The chain's middle link: love (agape, G26) is the pleroma (G4138) of nomos (G3551). This connects the faith-love link (Gal 5:6) to the love-law link — establishing that faith's operation through love results in law-keeping, not law-breaking.

verses.heading: Fourth link — law's righteousness fulfilled in Spirit-walkers
verses.kjv_text: That the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit.
verses.context: After establishing condemnation for sin (8:1-3), Paul states the PURPOSE: that the dikaioma (righteous requirement) of the law might be fulfilled IN believers.
verses.notes: Key grammatical feature: plerothe (V-APS-3S, aorist passive subjunctive) — divine passive. God fulfills the law's requirement IN believers. This is not human self-effort but divine enablement. The en hemin ('in us') shows the location is within believers. The contrast kata sarka / kata pneuma shows the mechanism: Spirit-empowered, not flesh-powered.

verses.heading: Fifth link — faith establishes the law (with me genoito pattern)
verses.kjv_text: Do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid: yea, we establish the law.
verses.context: After establishing justification by faith (3:21-30), Paul addresses the implied antinomian conclusion. His answer: me genoito (God forbid) — faith ESTABLISHES (histemi) the law.
verses.notes: The me genoito formula: Paul's emphatic denial. He refuses to draw the antinomian conclusion. Histanomen (we establish) is present active indicative — an ongoing reality, not a one-time event. This is the fifth link in the chain: faith -> love -> law fulfilled -> Spirit-empowered -> faith establishes law.

verses.heading: Identical phrase 'commandments of God' while excluding circumcision
verses.kjv_text: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.
verses.context: Paul's discussion of remaining in one's calling. Uses the identical phrase entolas theou while explicitly stating circumcision (ceremonial) is nothing.
verses.notes: Greek: teresis (G5084, N-NSF — cognate noun of tereo G5083) entolon (G1785, N-GPF — genitive plural) Theou (G2316, N-GSM). The cognate noun teresis directly parallels tereo in Rev 14:12. The phrase explicitly excludes circumcision — marking entolas theou as moral, not ceremonial. Parallel to Gal 5:6: same frame 'neither circumcision nor uncircumcision but X.' In Gal 5:6 X = 'faith working by love'; here X = 'keeping the commandments of God.' Paul treats these as equivalent.

verses.heading: Abraham's faith counted as righteousness — faith FIRST
verses.kjv_text: And he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness.
verses.context: After God promises descendants like the stars. Abraham's faith response is counted (chashab) as righteousness. This is the foundational OT text on justification by faith, cited by Paul (Rom 4:3), James (2:23), and Galatians (3:6).
verses.notes: Hebrew: he'emin (H539, Hiphil perfect 3ms of aman = 'he believed') + tsedaqah (H6666 = 'righteousness'). The chronological ordering is critical: faith (Gen 15:6) precedes obedience (Gen 26:5) — Abraham believed FIRST, obeyed AFTER. Three NT authors cite this text, establishing it as the paradigmatic faith-righteousness text.

verses.heading: Abraham's comprehensive obedience vocabulary — obedience AFTER
verses.kjv_text: Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.
verses.context: God explains to Isaac why he is blessing him — because of Abraham's obedience. This comes AFTER Genesis 15:6 chronologically.
verses.notes: Hebrew parsing confirms four distinct obedience terms: mishmarti (H4931, fs + 1cs suffix — my charge/guard), mitsvotai (H4687, fp + 1cs suffix — my commandments), chuqqotai (H2706/H2708, fp + 1cs suffix — my statutes), torotai (H8451, fp + 1cs suffix — my laws/instructions). The verb shamar (H8104, Qal wayyiqtol 3ms — 'and he kept') governs all four objects. The comprehensive vocabulary shows Abraham's obedience was total, not selective.

verses.heading: Faith worked with works — Abraham pattern in James
verses.kjv_text: Seest thou how faith wrought with his works, and by works was faith made perfect? And the scripture was fulfilled which saith, Abraham believed God, and it was imputed unto him for righteousness: and he was called the Friend of God.
verses.context: James argues faith and works are complementary, not competing. He cites Gen 15:6 but shows faith was 'completed' (teleioo) by works.
verses.notes: Key terms: synergei (V-IAI-3S — 'was working together with'), eteleiothe (V-API-3S — 'was made perfect/completed'). Faith and works cooperate (synergeo) and faith is brought to completion (teleioo) by works. James cites Gen 15:6 just as Paul does — same text, complementary applications. The order: faith is the root, works are the fruit that completes/demonstrates it.

verses.heading: By faith Abraham OBEYED — faith and obedience unified
verses.kjv_text: By faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, obeyed; and he went out, not knowing whither he went.
verses.context: Hebrews 11 faith catalogue. Faith and obedience appear in a single clause — Abraham obeyed BY faith.
verses.notes: Pistei (N-DSF, dative of means — 'by faith') Abraam (proper name) kaloumenos (V-PPP-NSM — 'being called') hupekousen (V-AAI-3S — aorist active indicative of hupakouo G5219 — 'he obeyed'). Faith is the INSTRUMENT of obedience, not its competitor. The dative of means shows faith is HOW he obeyed.

verses.heading: Opening of hupakoe pisteos inclusio — obedience of faith
verses.kjv_text: By whom we have received grace and apostleship, for obedience to the faith among all nations, for his name:
verses.context: Paul's opening statement of purpose in Romans. The phrase hupakoen pisteos frames the entire letter.
verses.notes: Greek: eis hupakoen (N-ASF, accusative — 'unto obedience') pisteos (N-GSF, genitive — 'of faith'). The genitive pisteos is deliberately multi-layered: (1) appositional: the obedience which IS faith, (2) source: the obedience which springs FROM faith. Garlington argues this is unique in pre-Christian Greek literature — a deliberate Pauline coinage. The phrase appears at both the opening (1:5) and closing (16:26) of Romans, forming an inclusio that frames the entire letter's argument.

verses.heading: Closing of hupakoe pisteos inclusio — identical phrase
verses.kjv_text: But now is made manifest, and by the scriptures of the prophets, according to the commandment of the everlasting God, made known to all nations for the obedience of faith:
verses.context: Paul's closing doxology. The same phrase eis hupakoen pisteos reappears, closing the inclusio opened in 1:5.
verses.notes: Identical construction: eis hupakoen (N-ASF) pisteos (N-GSF). The repetition at both ends of Romans is deliberate — the entire letter is framed by 'obedience of faith.' The genitive pisteos here carries the same multi-layered meaning as in 1:5. The fact that Romans — the most systematic treatment of justification by faith — is framed by 'obedience of faith' shows Paul did not see faith and obedience as competing.

verses.heading: Servants of obedience unto righteousness
verses.kjv_text: Know ye not, that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom ye obey; whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness? But God be thanked, that ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine which was delivered you. Being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness.
verses.context: Paul's argument about slavery — either to sin or to obedience. Freedom from sin leads to servanthood of righteousness through obedience.
verses.notes: The binary: douloi hamartias (servants of sin) vs. hupekousate (you obeyed, aorist active indicative) from the heart. Obedience is the response to liberation, not the cause. The sequence: freed from sin (v.18) -> servants of righteousness. Obedience flows FROM freedom, not toward it.

verses.heading: Paul quotes tenth commandment and calls it entole — decisive test case
verses.kjv_text: What shall we say then? [Is] the law sin? God forbid. Nay, I had not known sin, but by the law: for I had not known lust, except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet. But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence. For without the law sin [was] dead. For I was alive without the law once: but when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died. And the commandment, which [was ordained] to life, I found [to be] unto death. For sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me, and by it slew [me]. Wherefore the law [is] holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good.
verses.context: Paul explains the relationship between law, sin, and death. He quotes 'Thou shalt not covet' (tenth commandment) and calls it 'the commandment' (he entole) throughout vv.8-12.
verses.notes: This is the DECISIVE test case against the entole ≠ Decalogue claim. Paul quotes the tenth commandment ('thou shalt not covet' = Exo 20:17) and calls it he entole (G1785 with definite article) five times in vv.8-12. The articular singular (he entole = THE commandment) marks this as a specific, identifiable command — and it is unambiguously Decalogue content. The Greek parsing of v.8 confirms: tes entoles (N-GSF, genitive singular with definite article).

verses.heading: Fifth commandment called entole — second test case
verses.kjv_text: Honour thy father and mother; (which is the first commandment with promise;)
verses.context: Paul quotes the fifth commandment and explicitly calls it 'the first commandment (entole) with promise.'
verses.notes: Paul calls the fifth Decalogue commandment (honour father and mother) entole prote en epangelia — 'first commandment with promise.' This is the second test case proving entole = Decalogue content. The Tinker/Answering Adventism claim that entole = Jesus's teachings only cannot account for Paul's usage here.

verses.heading: Sabbath observance described as 'according to the commandment (entole)'
verses.kjv_text: And they returned, and prepared spices and ointments; and rested the sabbath day according to the commandment.
verses.context: After Jesus' death and burial. The women rest on the Sabbath kata ten entolen — 'according to THE commandment.'
verses.notes: Third test case: Sabbath observance is described as kata ten entolen (according to THE commandment). The definite article (ten) marks this as a specific, known commandment — the fourth commandment of the Decalogue. This directly links entole to Sabbath-keeping.

verses.heading: Spirit-enablement — God CAUSES obedience
verses.kjv_text: And I will put my spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes, and ye shall keep my judgments, and do [them].
verses.context: New covenant promise. God promises to put his Spirit within his people and CAUSE them to walk in his statutes. The obedience is God-powered, not self-powered.
verses.notes: The Hebrew ve'asiti ('and I will cause/make') has God as the subject — He is the causer of obedience. This eliminates the legalism charge: if God causes the obedience through his Spirit, it cannot be self-effort. The statutes (chuqqot) and judgments (mishpatim) are the same law content, now empowered by the Spirit.

verses.heading: God works both willing and doing — divine enablement
verses.kjv_text: For it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of [his] good pleasure.
verses.context: After 'work out your own salvation with fear and trembling' (v.12), Paul immediately clarifies: it is GOD working IN you.
verses.notes: Theos (God) is the subject of energon (V-PAP-NSM, present active participle — 'the one working'). God works (energeo) BOTH the willing (to thelein) AND the doing (to energein). The order: divine enablement (v.13) grounds human obedience (v.12). This is not legalism — it is theocentric sanctification.

verses.heading: Grace TEACHES denial of ungodliness — pedagogical grace
verses.kjv_text: For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly, in this present world;
verses.context: Paul describes grace not merely as forensic declaration but as active teacher (paideuo) of godly living.
verses.notes: Key word: paideuousa (V-PAP-NSF, present active participle — 'teaching/training'). Grace is the SUBJECT of the teaching verb. Grace trains believers to deny ungodliness and live righteously. Grace does not merely declare righteous — it produces righteousness. This is the pedagogical function of grace that answers the legalism charge.

verses.heading: Saved by grace through faith, created unto good works — cause vs. fruit
verses.kjv_text: For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: [it is] the gift of God: Not of works, lest any man should boast. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them.
verses.context: The paradigm text for the cause vs. fruit distinction. Works EXCLUDED as cause (v.9), AFFIRMED as purpose (v.10).

greek_parsing.gloss: Perf Pass Ptcp Nom Pl M

verses.notes: Greek parsing reveals the critical distinction: v.9 ouk ex ergon (N-GPN — 'not out of works') — works excluded as SOURCE/CAUSE of salvation. v.10 epi ergois agathois (N-DPN A-DPN — 'upon/unto good works') — works affirmed as PURPOSE/DESTINATION. The preposition epi + dative = purpose/basis. God before ordained (proetoimasen, V-AAI-3S) these works — they are divine purpose, not human initiative. Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping falls in the v.10 category (fruit/purpose), not the v.9 category (cause).

verses.heading: Pisteuo vs. apeitheo as direct antonyms — belief-obedience unity
verses.kjv_text: He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life: and he that believeth not the Son shall not see life; but the wrath of God abideth on him.
verses.context: John the Baptist's testimony about Jesus. The contrast is between pisteuo (believe) and apeitheo (disbelieve/disobey).

greek_parsing.gloss: Pres Act Ptcp Nom Sg M
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Sg M

verses.notes: Critical parsing: ho pisteuon (V-PAP-NSM — present active participle of pisteuo G4100, 'the one believing') vs. ho apeithon (V-PAP-NSM — present active participle of apeitheo G544, 'the one disbelieving/disobeying'). The KJV translates apeithon as 'believeth not,' but the word fundamentally means 'disobey' — it carries both senses. The antonym of belief IS disobedience, showing the biblical languages do not separate these concepts.

verses.heading: OT template — Fear God + keep commandments + judgment
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this [is] the whole [duty] of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether [it be] good, or whether [it be] evil.
verses.context: Solomon's conclusion to Ecclesiastes. The triple formula: fear God + keep commandments + judgment. Maps directly onto Rev 14:7,12.
verses.notes: The structural parallel with Rev 14:7,12: Eccl 12:13 ('Fear God + keep commandments') = Rev 14:7 ('Fear God') + Rev 14:12 ('keep commandments'). Eccl 12:14 ('God shall bring every work into judgment') = Rev 14:7 ('the hour of his judgment is come'). The temple formula from the OT reappears in the eschatological setting of Revelation.

verses.heading: Fourth commandment rationale echoed in Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: For [in] six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them [is], and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.context: The rationale clause of the fourth commandment. Four creation terms (made + heaven + earth + sea) reappear in Rev 14:7.
verses.notes: The four-term creation formula: asah (made/H6213) + shamayim (heaven) + erets (earth) + yam (sea). In the LXX: epoiesen (G4160, aorist active indicative of poieo) + ton ouranon + ten gen + ten thalassan. Rev 14:7 echoes this with the same verb poieo (not ktizo) + ouranos + ge + thalassa. The deliberate choice of poieo over ktizo in Rev 14:7 marks a direct allusion to the Sabbath commandment.

verses.heading: First me genoito denial — shall we continue in sin?
verses.kjv_text: What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound? God forbid. How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein?
verses.context: Paul addresses the first antinomian objection: if grace abounds where sin increases, should we keep sinning? His answer: me genoito.
verses.notes: Me genoito = 'may it not be!' / 'God forbid!' — Paul's strongest negative formula. The implied antinomian conclusion (more sin = more grace) is emphatically rejected. Paul's refusal to draw this conclusion demonstrates that justification by faith does not lead to lawlessness.

verses.heading: Second me genoito denial — shall we sin because under grace?
verses.kjv_text: What then? shall we sin, because we are not under the law, but under grace? God forbid.
verses.context: Second antinomian objection: if we are under grace not law, does that permit sin? Same emphatic denial.
verses.notes: Another me genoito. Paul refuses the conclusion that being 'under grace' permits sin. The wall of me genoito denials (Rom 3:31, 6:1-2, 6:15, Gal 2:17, 3:21) shows the apostle to the Gentiles — the champion of justification by faith — was simultaneously the most emphatic defender of the law's continuing moral authority.

verses.heading: Third me genoito denial — is Christ the minister of sin?
verses.kjv_text: But if, while we seek to be justified by Christ, we ourselves also are found sinners, [is] therefore Christ the minister of sin? God forbid.
verses.context: Paul rejects the idea that seeking justification by faith makes Christ a servant of sin.
verses.notes: Another me genoito. Paul refuses to allow justification by faith to be construed as license for sin.

verses.heading: Fourth me genoito denial — is the law against the promises?
verses.kjv_text: [Is] the law then against the promises of God? God forbid: for if there had been a law given which could have given life, verily righteousness should have been by the law.
verses.context: Paul denies that law and promises are in opposition. They serve different functions but do not contradict.
verses.notes: Me genoito again. The law is not AGAINST (kata) God's promises. They are not competing systems. This is the same point Rev 14:12 makes visually: commandments AND faith, not commandments OR faith.

verses.heading: Overcomers sing song of Moses AND the Lamb — same group as 14:12
verses.kjv_text: And I saw as it were a sea of glass mingled with fire: and them that had gotten the victory over the beast, and over his image, and over his mark, [and] over the number of his name, stand on the sea of glass, having the harps of God. And they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, Great and marvellous [are] thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true [are] thy ways, thou King of saints.
verses.context: The overcomers of the beast crisis. They sing TWO songs: Moses (law) AND the Lamb (gospel). The dual song parallels the dual formula of 14:12.
verses.notes: The nikao-hupomone relationship: those who endure in 14:12 are the nikao (overcoming) saints of 15:2. Their song combines Moses (representing the law/commandments) and the Lamb (representing faith/gospel). Law and gospel united in one hymn — the same unity Rev 14:12 describes.

verses.heading: Ark contained the Decalogue tablets — OT confirmation
verses.kjv_text: At that time the LORD said unto me, Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first, and come up unto me into the mount, and make thee an ark of wood. And I will write on the tables the words that were in the first tables which thou brakest, and thou shalt put them in the ark. And I made an ark [of] shittim wood, and hewed two tables of stone like unto the first, and went up into the mount, having the two tables in mine hand. And he wrote on the tables, according to the first writing, the ten commandments, which the LORD spake unto you in the mount out of the midst of the fire in the day of the assembly: and the LORD gave them unto me. And I turned myself and came down from the mount, and put the tables in the ark which I had made; and there they be, as the LORD commanded me.
verses.context: Moses recounts placing the Decalogue tablets in the ark. This establishes what the ark contained — exclusively the 'ten commandments.'
verses.notes: Supporting verse for the commandment inclusio. When Rev 11:19 reveals the ark in heaven, the reader knows its contents: the Decalogue.

verses.heading: Nothing in the ark save the two tables — Decalogue exclusively
verses.kjv_text: [There was] nothing in the ark save the two tables of stone, which Moses put there at Horeb, when the LORD made [a covenant] with the children of Israel, when they came out of the land of Egypt.
verses.context: Solomon's temple dedication. Confirms the ark contained ONLY the two stone tablets — the Decalogue.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The emphatic 'nothing in the ark save' (ein ba-aron raq) confirms exclusive Decalogue content.

verses.heading: Jesus: love me → keep my commandments
verses.kjv_text: If ye love me, keep my commandments.
verses.context: Jesus connects love and commandment-keeping in the farewell discourse.
verses.notes: Supporting verse for the faith-love-law chain. Love (agapao) is the motive; keeping commandments (tereo + entolas) is the expression.

verses.heading: Jesus kept Father's commandments — model pattern
verses.kjv_text: If ye keep my commandments, ye shall abide in my love; even as I have kept my Father's commandments, and abide in his love.
verses.context: Jesus presents himself as the model: He kept the Father's commandments. The pattern flows from Christ to disciples.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Jesus' own commandment-keeping (ego tetireka, perfect active indicative — settled completed state) models the pattern for believers.

verses.heading: Knowing God = keeping commandments; denying = liar
verses.kjv_text: And hereby we do know that we know him, if we keep his commandments. He that saith, I know him, and keepeth not his commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in him.
verses.context: John's test of genuine knowledge of God: commandment-keeping. Failure to keep = claiming to know God while being a liar.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Uses tereo + entolas (same vocabulary as Rev 14:12). Commandment-keeping is the EVIDENCE of knowing God, not the CAUSE.

verses.heading: Keep commandments + believe in Jesus — both together
verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever we ask, we receive of him, because we keep his commandments, and do those things that are pleasing in his sight. And this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ, and love one another, as he gave us commandment. And he that keepeth his commandments dwelleth in him, and he in him. And hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us.
verses.context: John combines commandment-keeping with believing in Jesus — the same dual formula as Rev 14:12.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The dual command in v.23: (1) believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ AND (2) love one another. Commandment-keeping + faith in Jesus — identical to Rev 14:12's structure. Verse 24 adds the Spirit-enablement dimension.

verses.heading: By deeds of the law no flesh justified — works excluded as CAUSE
verses.kjv_text: Therefore by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight: for by the law [is] the knowledge of sin.
verses.context: Paul establishes that works of the law cannot justify — the law's function is to reveal sin, not to save.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Uses ex ergon nomou (out of works of law) — the specific phrase marking works as excluded CAUSE of justification. This is the 'erga nomou' category that Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping does NOT fall into.

verses.heading: Paul's use of Gen 15:6 — Abraham believed God
verses.kjv_text: For what saith the scripture? Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness.
verses.context: Paul cites Gen 15:6 to establish that righteousness comes by faith, not works.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Paul's citation of Gen 15:6 establishes the Abrahamic pattern for the Roman audience.

verses.heading: Faith counted BEFORE circumcision — temporal ordering
verses.kjv_text: How was it then reckoned? when he was in circumcision, or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision. And he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which [he had yet] being uncircumcised: that he might be the father of all them that believe, though they be not circumcised; that righteousness might be imputed unto them also:
verses.context: Paul establishes the chronological ordering: faith (Gen 15:6) came BEFORE circumcision (Gen 17). The sequence is faith → righteousness → sign.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The temporal ordering is critical for the Abrahamic pattern: faith FIRST (Gen 15:6), then circumcision (Gen 17), then comprehensive obedience (Gen 26:5).

verses.heading: Abraham believed God — Gentiles justified same pattern
verses.kjv_text: Even as Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness. Know ye therefore that they which are of faith, the same are the children of Abraham. And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, [saying], In thee shall all nations be blessed. So then they which be of faith are blessed with faithful Abraham.
verses.context: Paul extends the Abrahamic faith pattern to Gentiles. Those 'of faith' (ek pisteos) are Abraham's children.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The Abrahamic pattern is universal: faith → righteousness for all nations, not just Israel.

verses.heading: Jesus: not come to destroy but to fulfill — law's continuation
verses.kjv_text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil. For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled. Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called the least in the kingdom of heaven: but whosoever shall do and teach [them], the same shall be called great in the kingdom of heaven.
verses.context: Jesus explicitly denies abolishing the law. The law continues 'till heaven and earth pass.'
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Jesus' own statement about the law's continuation. Note entole in v.19 referring to law-commandments that must be kept and taught.

verses.heading: Paul ennomos Christou — 'in-lawed to Christ'
verses.kjv_text: To them that are without law, as without law, (being not without law to God, but under the law to Christ,) that I might gain them that are without law.
verses.context: Paul clarifies his own position: he is NOT anomos (lawless) but ennomos (in-lawed) to Christ.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The en- prefix of ennomos directly opposes the a- prefix of anomos. Paul is WITHIN the law's framework through Christ, not outside it.

verses.heading: Thyatira commended for works, love, service, faith, patience
verses.kjv_text: I know thy works, and charity, and service, and faith, and thy patience, and thy works; and the last [to be] more than the first.
verses.context: Jesus commends Thyatira for their works, love, faith, and patience — the same constellation of terms found in Rev 14:12.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The combination of erga + agape + pistis + hupomone mirrors Rev 14:12's description.

verses.heading: Kept word of patience — tereo for keeping word
verses.kjv_text: Because thou hast kept the word of my patience, I also will keep thee from the hour of temptation, which shall come upon all the world, to try them that dwell upon the earth.
verses.context: Philadelphia commended for keeping (tereo) the word of patience (hupomone). Same vocabulary as 14:12.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Uses tereo (G5083) for keeping — same verb as Rev 14:12. The 'word of my patience' (ton logon tes hupomones mou) connects to the hupomone of 14:12.

verses.heading: Abraham's faith tested through offering Isaac
verses.kjv_text: By faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered up Isaac: and he that had received the promises offered up his only begotten [son], Of whom it was said, That in Isaac shall thy seed be called: Accounting that God [was] able to raise [him] up, even from the dead; from whence also he received him in a figure.
verses.context: Abraham's faith demonstrated through the supreme test — offering Isaac. Faith expressed AS obedience.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Pistei (by faith) + prosenenoken (offered up) — faith as the instrument of Abraham's radical obedience.

verses.heading: Christ the telos of the law — goal, not termination
verses.kjv_text: For Christ [is] the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.
verses.context: Paul identifies Christ as the telos (goal/purpose) of the law. Prior study gsp-04 established telos = goal, not cessation.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Telos (G5056) = goal/purpose in 90% of NT usage. Christ is the goal the law points toward, not its terminator.

verses.heading: New covenant — law written on hearts, same content new surface
verses.kjv_text: But this [shall be] the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel; After those days, saith the LORD, I will put my law in their inward parts, and write it in their hearts; and will be their God, and they shall be my people.
verses.context: The new covenant promise. God writes the SAME law (towrah) on hearts — internalization, not abolition.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The new covenant does not replace the law's content but changes its location (from stone tablets to hearts) and power source (from external to Spirit-internal). Same law, new surface.

verses.heading: NT citation of Jer 31:33 — law in minds and hearts
verses.kjv_text: For this [is] the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, saith the Lord; I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts: and I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people:
verses.context: The author of Hebrews quotes Jer 31:33, confirming the new covenant's internalization of the law.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The NT citation uses nomous (G3551, laws) — confirming the law content continues in the new covenant.

verses.heading: Love God + keep commandments = mutual proof
verses.kjv_text: By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God, and keep his commandments.
verses.context: John connects loving God and keeping commandments as mutual evidence of loving God's children.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Love and commandment-keeping are inseparable evidence of authentic faith.

verses.heading: Excluded sins correspond to Decalogue violations
verses.kjv_text: For without [are] dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie.
verses.context: Those excluded from the city. The sins listed correspond to Decalogue violations: murder (6th), sexual immorality (7th), idolatry (1st-2nd), lying (9th).
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The excluded sins correspond to specific Decalogue commandments, confirming the Decalogue as the moral standard of Revelation's eschatology.

verses.heading: Pistis Iesou Christou — Pauline parallel to Rev 14:12
verses.kjv_text: Even the righteousness of God [which is] by faith of Jesus Christ unto all and upon all them that believe: for there is no difference:
verses.context: Paul uses the pistis Iesou Christou construction — structurally identical genitive ambiguity as Rev 14:12.
verses.notes: Supporting verse for the pistis Iesou genitive question. Same construction as Rev 14:12. Hays' redundancy argument: if pistis Christou = 'faith in Christ,' then 'unto all them that believe' becomes redundant.

verses.heading: Pistis Christou Iesou — justification by faith of Christ
verses.kjv_text: Knowing that a man is not justified by the works of the law, but by the faith of Jesus Christ, even we have believed in Jesus Christ, that we might be justified by the faith of Christ, and not by the works of the law: for by the works of the law shall no flesh be justified.
verses.context: Paul's clearest statement on justification. Uses pistis Christou twice alongside pisteuo eis Christon — distinguishing the genitive construction from the 'believe in' construction.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The coexistence of pisteos Christou Iesou AND episteuamen eis Christon Iesoun in the same verse suggests they are not identical — supporting the subjective genitive reading of pistis Christou as Christ's own faithfulness.

verses.heading: Righteousness through the faith of Christ
verses.kjv_text: And be found in him, not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith:
verses.context: Paul contrasts his own law-righteousness with the righteousness that comes through pistis Christou.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Another Pauline pistis Christou construction for comparison with Rev 14:12.

verses.heading: Pergamos held fast my name, did not deny my faith
verses.kjv_text: I know thy works, and where thou dwellest, [even] where Satan's seat [is]: and thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith, even in those days wherein Antipas [was] my faithful martyr, who was slain among you, where Satan dwelleth.
verses.context: Jesus commends Pergamos for holding his name and not denying his faith (ten pistin mou). The genitive mou (my) suggests subjective: Jesus' own faith/faithfulness.
verses.notes: Supporting verse for the subjective genitive reading. Ten pistin mou = 'my faith' — Jesus' own faithfulness that the saints hold to. This supports reading pistin Iesou in 14:12 as Jesus' faithfulness.

verses.heading: Promise by faith of Jesus Christ given to believers
verses.kjv_text: But the scripture hath concluded all under sin, that the promise by faith of Jesus Christ might be given to them that believe.
verses.context: The promise comes through pistis Iesou Christou to 'them that believe' (tois pisteuousin) — distinguishing the genitive from the verb.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Same redundancy pattern as Rom 3:22 — if pistis Iesou Christou = 'faith in Jesus Christ,' then 'to them that believe' is redundant.

verses.heading: Grace produces a people zealous of good works
verses.kjv_text: Who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity, and purify unto himself a peculiar people, zealous of good works.
verses.context: Christ's redemptive purpose: to create a people zealous (zeloten) of good works (kalon ergon). Works as divine PURPOSE, not human cause.
verses.notes: Supporting verse for the cause vs. fruit distinction. Redemption (cause) produces zealousness for good works (fruit).

verses.heading: Believers maintain good works — faith produces action
verses.kjv_text: [This is] a faithful saying, and these things I will that thou affirm constantly, that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works. These things are good and profitable unto men.
verses.context: Paul connects believing in God with maintaining good works — faith is the root, works the fruit.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Believers (hoi pepisteuikotes theo) maintain (proistasthai) good works (kalon ergon). The perfect tense pepisteuikotes = 'those who have believed and continue believing' — settled faith produces ongoing works.

verses.heading: First angel echoes Exo 20:11 — worship him that made
verses.text: Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.notes: The creation formula (him that made heaven, earth, sea, fountains) echoes Exo 20:11 (heaven, earth, sea, all in them) — linking the first angel to the fourth commandment/Sabbath. This connects "commandments of God" in 14:12 to "worship the Creator" in 14:7.

greek_parsing.gloss: Aor Act Ptcp Dat Sg M

word_studies.summary: Commandment, precept. 65 unique verses (71 tokens). Rendered 'commandment(s)' in 93.8% of verses. Johannine concentration is the defining feature (34% of uses). Key semantic categories: (1) Decalogue/Mosaic Law, (2) Christ's new commandment of love, (3) Father's commandment to Christ, (4) Keeping commandments as knowing/loving God, (5) Commandment/sin/death (Pauline), (6) Human traditions vs. God's commandment, (7) Priestly/Levitical commandment. Collocates strongly with theos (14x, 'commandments of God') and tereo (14x, 'keep commandments'). NT vocabulary partition: entole = moral commands, dogma = abolished, entalma = human precepts.
word_studies.relevance: Core word in Rev 14:12. The entole = Decalogue pattern (43/43 unqualified instances) and the three decisive test cases (Rom 7:7-12, Eph 6:2, Luke 23:56) establish that tas entolas tou theou = the Decalogue.

word_studies.summary: Faith, faithfulness, belief. 244 occurrences across 222 KJV translation instances. Overwhelmingly Pauline — Romans, Galatians, and Pastorals dominate. Semantic categories: (1) active trust/confidence in God, (2) justifying faith (means of righteousness), (3) the body of belief ('the faith'), (4) faithfulness/fidelity, (5) exemplary faith (Hebrews 11), (6) assurance/proof, (7) spiritual gift. Collocates strongly with theos (52x), Iesous (40x), Christos (36x), agape (24x). The pistis Christou genitive construction is the most debated subjective/objective genitive in NT scholarship.
word_studies.relevance: Core word in Rev 14:12 — 'the faith of Jesus.' The genitive construction (pistis + personal name in genitive) is structurally ambiguous between subjective (Jesus' faithfulness) and objective (faith in Jesus). The grammar reference entry on subjective/objective genitive provides the analytical framework.

word_studies.summary: Keep, guard, observe. 75 occurrences. Dominant usage: faithful, active compliance with a divine charge — keeping commandments, words, and law. Strong Johannine concentration (John 18, Revelation 11, 1 John 8). Collocates with entole (14x, 'keep commandments') and logos (15x, 'keep my word'). Distinct from phylasso (preventing escape, defensive containment) — tereo = watchful, attentive, active surveillance and fidelity. Hebrew sources: natsar H5341 (8x, PMI 7.27) and shamar H8104 (10x, PMI 4.78).
word_studies.relevance: Core verb in Rev 14:12 — 'they that KEEP (terountes) the commandments.' The present active participle form (terountes) characterizes the saints by ongoing habitual activity — descriptive, not prescriptive.

word_studies.summary: Law. 197 occurrences. Heavily concentrated in Paul (Romans 74, Galatians 32). ABSENT from Revelation entirely. Semantic categories: (1) Mosaic Law/Torah, (2) OT Scriptures broadly, (3) principle/operative rule, (4) law of Christ/royal law/law of liberty, (5) natural/moral law, (6) law of commandments/ordinances. The absence of nomos from Revelation is significant: John uses entole (G1785) exclusively for the commandment vocabulary in Revelation. The Tinker/Answering Adventism claim that entole ≠ Decalogue because nomos = the Law fails when tested against Paul's usage (Rom 7:7-12, Eph 6:2).
word_studies.relevance: The nomos/entole distinction is central to the study. Nomos is ABSENT from Revelation; John uses entole exclusively. The critic argument that entole ≠ Decalogue because nomos = the Law is refuted by Paul's own usage where he calls Decalogue commands entole.

word_studies.summary: Obedience, attentive hearkening. 14 unique verses (15 tokens). Etymologically hupo + akouo = hearing as a subordinate. Concentrated in Paul (Romans 6, 2 Corinthians 3, Philemon 1). Semantic categories: (1) obedience to gospel/faith, (2) Christ's obedience as redemptive counterpart, (3) obedience vs. disobedience, (4) practical submission, (5) sanctification through obedience to truth. The hupakoen pisteos construction (Rom 1:5, 16:26) frames all of Romans — a deliberate Pauline coinage expressing both 'the obedience which IS faith' and 'the obedience which springs FROM faith.'
word_studies.relevance: The hupakoe pisteos inclusio (Rom 1:5/16:26) is the conceptual bridge between Rev 14:12's twin marks. Garlington argues this is unique to pre-Christian Greek literature — a deliberate Pauline coinage showing faith and obedience are inseparable.

word_studies.summary: Love. 116 tokens in 106 unique verses across 24 books. Overwhelmingly Pauline and Johannine. Semantic categories: (1) God's love toward humanity, (2) God's essential nature as love, (3) Christ's love, (4) mutual love among believers, (5) supreme virtue/greatest gift, (6) love as obedience/walking in commandments, (7) love in virtue lists. Critical for the faith-love-law chain: Gal 5:6 (faith works by love) → 1 John 5:3 (love = keeping commandments) → Rom 13:10 (love fulfills the law).
word_studies.relevance: Bridge between faith and commandments in the faith-love-law chain. Agape is the medium through which faith operates (Gal 5:6) and the content of commandment-keeping (1 John 5:3).

word_studies.summary: Grace, favor, thanks. 147 unique verses (156 tokens). Dominantly Pauline (~80 of 147 verses). Semantic categories: (1) epistolary greeting, (2) divine saving grace/unmerited favor, (3) ministry grace for service, (4) social favor, (5) gratitude/thanks, (6) epistolary benediction. Key for legalism objection: Titus 2:11-12 shows grace as PEDAGOGICAL — grace teaches denial of ungodliness and godly living. Grace produces, not prevents, obedience.
word_studies.relevance: Addresses the legalism charge. Grace is not opposed to obedience — it produces it. Titus 2:11-12 (grace teaches godly living) and Rom 6:15 (grace does not permit sin) together answer the claim that commandment-keeping = legalism.

word_studies.summary: Believe, trust, entrust. 220 unique verses (264 tokens). John dominates with 86/220 verses (39%). Present active participle (ho pisteuon, 'the one believing') accounts for 22% of forms — ongoing faith. Semantic categories: (1) saving faith in Christ, (2) belief in God's word/promises, (3) intellectual acknowledgment, (4) ongoing trust and reliance, (5) refusal to believe, (6) entrusting. Antonym: apeitheo G544 (disbelieve/disobey) — John 3:36 sets them as direct antonyms.
word_studies.relevance: Verb form of pistis. The pisteuo/apeitheo antonym pair in John 3:36 shows the biblical languages do not separate belief from obedience.

word_studies.summary: Work, deed, action. 161 unique verses (176 tokens). Heaviest in Pastoral Epistles, James, and Revelation. Two critical vocabulary categories: (1) erga nomou ('works of the law') — works excluded as CAUSE of justification (Rom 3:20,28; Gal 2:16; 3:2,5,10); (2) erga agatha ('good works') — works affirmed as PURPOSE/FRUIT (Eph 2:10; Titus 2:14; 3:1,8). The distinction is visible only in Greek — English 'works' collapses both categories.
semantic_categories: works of the law (excluded as cause)
good works of believers (affirmed as fruit)
word_studies.relevance: The erga nomou vs. erga agatha distinction is critical for answering the legalism charge. Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping falls in the fruit/purpose category (erga agatha), not the cause category (erga nomou).

word_studies.summary: Faithful, trustworthy, believing. 62 unique verses (67 tokens). Two senses: active (one who believes/trusts) and passive (one who is trustworthy/reliable). Applied to God/Christ as divine title ('God is faithful,' 'Faithful and True'). The 'faithful sayings' formula in Pastorals. In Revelation, Jesus is called 'Faithful and True' (Rev 19:11) — supporting the subjective genitive reading of pistis Iesou as Jesus' own faithfulness.
word_studies.relevance: Adjective from pistis. Jesus is called pistos in Revelation (19:11, 'Faithful and True') — supporting the reading of pistis Iesou in 14:12 as Jesus' faithfulness.

word_studies.summary: Obey, heed. Verb form of hupakoe (G5218). Heb 11:8 uses it for Abraham: pistei Abraam kaloumenos hupekousen — 'By faith Abraham, being called, obeyed.' Faith is the instrument of obedience (dative of means).
word_studies.relevance: Verb form of hupakoe. Heb 11:8 unifies faith and obedience in a single clause: 'by faith Abraham obeyed.'

word_studies.summary: Disbelieve, disobey. The word carries BOTH meanings — the biblical languages do not separate unbelief from disobedience. In John 3:36, pisteuo and apeitheo are set as direct antonyms: 'he that believeth (ho pisteuon) the Son... he that believeth not (ho apeithon) the Son.' The KJV translates apeithon as 'believeth not,' but the word fundamentally means 'disobey.'
word_studies.relevance: Shows the belief-obedience unity in biblical thought. The antonym of pisteuo (believe) is apeitheo (disobey) — not merely 'disagree' or 'doubt.'

word_studies.summary: Make, do. Generic creation/action verb. In Rev 14:7, deliberately chosen over ktizo (G2936) for the creation formula — matching Exo 20:11 LXX where poieo renders asah (H6213). Rev 22:14 uses poieo for 'do his commandments' (poiountes tas entolas autou). The poieo/ktizo distinction in Revelation: generic creation language uses ktizo (Rev 4:11, 10:6); only Rev 14:7 uses poieo — the verb mapping to Exo 20:11 LXX.
word_studies.relevance: The deliberate choice of poieo over ktizo in Rev 14:7 links the first angel's message to the fourth commandment's creation rationale.

word_studies.summary: Here, in this place. Functions as apocalyptic pointer in Revelation — 'HERE it is, look.' The hode inclusio brackets the beast crisis: Rev 13:10 ('Here is the patience and the faith') opens; Rev 14:12 ('Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep...') closes. The hode + estin construction is declarative, not imperative — it DESCRIBES, does not PRESCRIBE.
word_studies.relevance: The hode + estin construction confirms Rev 14:12 as declarative description of the saints, not prescriptive command.

word_studies.summary: Decree, ordinance. NT vocabulary for abolished ceremonial regulations (Eph 2:15, Col 2:14). Distinguished from entole: dogma = ceremonial decrees that have been removed; entole = moral commandments that continue. The NT cessation vocabulary (dogma, cheirographon, skia) consistently refers to ceremonial regulations; the continuation vocabulary (entole, nomos unqualified) refers to moral law.
word_studies.relevance: The entole/dogma distinction: NT uses dogma for abolished ceremonial regulations and entole for continuing moral commands. No overlap.

word_studies.summary: Spirit, wind, breath. The Spirit-enablement mechanism: Ezek 36:27 (God causes obedience through Spirit), Rom 8:4 (law fulfilled in Spirit-walkers), Gal 5:22 (fruit of the Spirit). The Spirit is the power source for the obedience described in Rev 14:12 — eliminating the legalism charge.
word_studies.relevance: Spirit-enablement eliminates the legalism charge. If God causes obedience through the Spirit, it cannot be self-powered legalism.

word_studies.summary: Persuade, trust, obey. Root verb behind pistis (G4102). Semantic range spans from persuade to trust to obey — showing the conceptual continuity between belief and obedience in the pistis word family.
word_studies.relevance: Root of pistis. The semantic range of peitho (persuade → trust → obey) shows that pistis-family words inherently include an obedience dimension.

word_studies.summary: Believe, trust, be firm. Hebrew root behind Gen 15:6 (he'emin = 'he believed'). The Hiphil form conveys putting trust in, relying upon. LXX renders with pisteuo (G4100).
word_studies.relevance: Abraham's belief in Gen 15:6 — the foundational OT faith text that establishes the faith-first pattern.

word_studies.summary: Keep, guard, watch over, observe. Hebrew verb governing the four obedience terms in Gen 26:5 (shamar mishmarti mitsvotai chuqqotai torotai). LXX renders with both tereo and phylasso. Cognate of mishmereth (H4931).
word_studies.relevance: The governing verb in Gen 26:5 — Abraham 'kept' (shamar) God's comprehensive charge. Connects to tereo in Rev 14:12 through LXX bridge.

word_studies.summary: Commandment. Hebrew equivalent of entole (G1785). One of four obedience terms in Gen 26:5 (mitsvotai = 'my commandments'). LXX renders with entole — establishing the direct translation bridge from OT 'commandments' to NT entole.
word_studies.relevance: Hebrew behind 'commandments' in Gen 26:5. The mitsvah → entole LXX bridge connects Abraham's obedience vocabulary to Rev 14:12's entolas tou theou.

word_studies.summary: Charge, guard, watch, duty. 78 occurrences. Cognate noun of shamar (H8104). Dominant usage: Levitical/priestly duty (Numbers 3-4 accounts for nearly half of all occurrences). Also used for guarding/watchkeeping, divine command/covenant obligation, and safekeeping/custody. In Gen 26:5, mishmereth represents the broadest, most comprehensive obedience term — the total body of obligations entrusted by God. Collocates strongly with shamar (30x, 'keep the charge' — cognate verb+noun intensifying the obligation), mitsvah (4x), towrah (3x), and chuqqah (3x).
word_studies.relevance: In Gen 26:5, mishmereth is the broadest obedience term — Abraham kept God's total 'charge.' The comprehensive vocabulary (mishmereth + mitsvot + chuqqot + torot) shows Abraham's obedience was exhaustive.

parallels.connection_type: vocabulary + structural (hupakoe pisteos inclusio — identical phrase)
vocabulary + theological (poieo + ouranos + ge + thalassa — creation formula echo)
structural + theological (fear God + keep commandments + judgment template)
